content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 5, 2006


Arnold W. Morrison
Delano Jt. Union High School Dist.
1747 Princeton Street
Delano, California 93215

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-157

Dear Mr. Morrison:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your question is
general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to
regulation 18329(c).² In addition, our response is limited to issues arising under the Act.
Nothing in the Act prohibits a member of the public from filing an application for
employment with a governmental agency. However, you may need to seek advice from
your own attorney regarding Government Code section 1090.

QUESTION

Does Suzanne Villaruz's application to be a school nurse in the Delano Joint
Union High School District result in a conflict of interest for you as a district board of
education trustee?

CONCLUSION

In order for the Commission to analyze your question, specific information is
needed regarding the governmental decision you are being asked to make as a public
official. While we do not have this information, this letter does provide you with general
guidance that may assist you in making a determination.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Pursuant to your August 2, 2006, facsimile, we have corrected the designation of the name of the
requestor in this letter to you.
³ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. I-06-157
Page No. 2

FACTS

You were elected to the board of education for the Delano Joint Union High
School District in November 2004. In May 2006, a school nurse position was posted
online for a high school under your board's jurisdiction.

In June 2006, your long-term partner (you are not married nor are you registered
domestic partners), Suzanne Villaruz, applied for the nursing position. You and Ms.
Villaruz have resided together for several years. While she is not on the title to your
residence, she is a co-signer on the loan for your home. You share all household
expenses equally.⁴

ANALYSIS

The Act was adopted by the voters of California in 1974. The purpose for the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether
elected or appointed, would "perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from any
bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them." (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

To determine whether a conflict exists, the Commission has developed an eight-
step process. (Regulation 18700, subdivisions (b)(1) - (8).) Because you have not
identified a specific governmental decision that will come before you, we are unable to
perform the analysis to determine if a conflict of interest exists. However, the following
review of the eight-step process may be used to offer guidance if a possible conflict
arises.

Steps 1 & 2: Are you a public official making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?

As an elected member of the Delano Joint Union High School District, you are a
public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, you may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use your official position to influence any decision
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest
you may have.

You "make a governmental decision" when you, acting within the authority of
your office or position, vote on a matter, obligate or commit your agency to any course of
action, or enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of your agency. (Regulation

_______________________
⁴ This is based on telephone conversations with Ms. Villaruz, which you authorized.

File No. I-06-157
Page No. 3

18702.1.) You "participate in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of your position and without significant substantive or intervening review, you
negotiate, advise, or make recommendations to the decision maker regarding the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) You attempt to use your official position
to "influence" a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, you contact or appear before
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of your agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)5

Step 3: What are your economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from economic interests. These economic interests are defined in section 87103 of the
Act and regulations 18703.1 - 18703.5, and are summarized below:

* A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

* A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);

* A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
time the relevant governmental decision was made (section 87103(c); regulation
18703.3). Although you and Ms. Villaruz jointly obtained a commercial loan for the
home in which you and Ms. Villaruz reside, and you share mortgage and household
expenses equally, you do not have an economic interest in Ms. Villaruz; she is not
a source of income to you. We have previously advised that when two unmarried
individuals who are involved in a bona fide dating relationship live together, the
mere fact of living together does not create an economic interest in the other. We
have also said that where there is a mere joint tenancy arrangement, no economic
interest is created between the joint tenants. (Biondo Advice Letter, No. A-86-
238; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-99-240.) However, if you receive income from
Ms. Villaruz, such as payments on a loan, the bona fide dating relationship
exception would not apply, and she would be an economic interest of yours.
(Albuquerque Advice Letter, No. A-00-120.);

5 If a public official is enumerated in section 87200 (including officials who manage public
investments) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5
subdivision (b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for
the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (§ 87105). If your position includes the authority to invest public funds,
these provisions apply to you.

File No. I-06-157
Page No. 4

* A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(e); regulation 18703.4). Based on previous advice this Commission has
provided, personal gifts shared between those in a bona fide dating relationship
would not count toward the $360 threshold. (Harvey Advice Letter, No. I-04-
097.) It appears that you and Ms. Villaruz's relationship qualifies for this bona
fide dating relationship exception;

* A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule
(section 87103; regulation 18703.5). "Immediate family" is defined in section
82029 as "...the spouse and dependent children." Regulation 18229 expanded
this definition by stating that "[f]or purposes of this title and implementing
regulations, the term 'spouse' shall include registered domestic partners
recognized by state law." Since Ms. Villaruz is not your spouse or registered
domestic partner, for purposes of the Act, she does not appear to qualify as an
immediate family member.

Absent an economic interest, it is not necessary to apply the remaining steps.
However, to provide a general discussion, the remaining steps involve the following:

Step 4: For each of your economic interests, determine whether that interest is
directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision that you will be making,
participating in making, or using or attempting to use your official position to influence.
(See regulation 18704.)

Step 5: Determine the applicable materiality standard for each economic interest,
based upon the degree of involvement determined pursuant to regulation 18704. (See
regulation 18705.)

Step 6: Determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests. If it is not
reasonably foreseeable that there will be such an effect on any of your economic
interests, you do not have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. (See
regulation 18707.)

Step 7: Determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally. If you can establish that such an effect on your
economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally, you do not
have a conflict of interest within the meaning of the Act. (See regulation 18707.)

Step 8: If a conflict of interest exists, there are limited circumstances when your
participation may be legally required despite your conflict of interest. (See regulation
18708.)

File No. I-06-157
Page No. 5

If you have any questions, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
